coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots modified CdSe loaded reduced graphene oxide for enhancing photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Huo, Pengwei
Guan, Jingru
Zhou, Mingjun
Ma, Changchang
Liu, Xinlin
Yan, Yongsheng
Yuan, Shouqi
coredata.dc:description:
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/CdSe/reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was fabricated by hydrothermal method. The structures, optical and electronic properties of as-obtained photocatalysts were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and photoelectrochemical testing. The photocatalytic performances were investigated by degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC·HCl). The results showed CQDs modified CdSe loaded rGO could effectively remove TC·HCl, the CdSe modified with 7mL CQDs showed higher photocatalytic degradation rate; the 5wt% loaded rGO for CQDs/CdSe showed highest photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic mechanism exhibited OH, O2
− and 1O2 radicals play key roles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CdSe QDs
rGO
Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalytic technology
Antibiotics

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation of CQDs-CdSe-rGO in the whole synthetic process.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the CdSe (black curve) and CQDs/CdSe-rGO (red curve, 5wt% GO).
TEM images of photocatalysts (a) CQDs, (b) CdSe, (c) CQDs/CdSe, (d) CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO).
UV–vis spectra of CdSe, QDs C/CdSe and QDs C/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO).
Solid fluorescence spectra of CdSe, CQDs/CdSe and CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO): excitated wavelength at 337nm.

Transient photocurrent responses of QDs CdSe, CQDs/CdSe and CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO) nanocomposite under visible light irradiation: using a standard three-electrode quartz cells with 0.5M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution, a Pt wire and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as the counter electrode and the reference electrodes, respectively.

20mg/L TC·HCl photodegradation efficiency of as-prepared CQDs/CdSe with different concentrations of CQDs (a) and the different contents of introduced rGO (b), the influence of different pH values of TC·HCl using the CQDs/CdSe-rGO 5wt% and 20mg/L TC·HCl (c), the influence of different concentrations of TC·HCl using the CQDs/CdSe-rGO 5 wt% (d), the kinetics analysis of degradation different concentrations TC·HCl (e), the recycled experiments using the CQDs/CdSe-rGO 5wt% and 20mg/L TC·HCl(f).
Analysis of active species using the CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO) by degradation 20mg/L TC·HCl with adding different scavengers in the photocatalytic processes.

DMPO and TEMP spin-trapping ESR spectra with 0.1mg/mL CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO) samples in the dark and under 450W xenon lamp solar simulator irradiation for 70s for DMPO-O2
− in methanol dispersion (a), DMPO-OH in aqueous dispersion (b), and TEMPONE-1O2 in aqueous dispersion (c).
Illustrations of CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO) in enhancing the separation of the photogenerated e−/h+ transfer process.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots modified CdSe loaded reduced graphene oxide for enhancing photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center of Fluid Machinery Engineering and Technology, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Fluid Machinery Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/CdSe/reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was fabricated by hydrothermal method. The structures, optical and electronic properties of as-obtained photocatalysts were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and photoelectrochemical testing. The photocatalytic performances were investigated by degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC·HCl). The results showed CQDs modified CdSe loaded rGO could effectively remove TC·HCl, the CdSe modified with 7mL CQDs showed higher photocatalytic degradation rate; the 5wt% loaded rGO for CQDs/CdSe showed highest photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic mechanism exhibited OH, O2
− and 1O2 radicals play key roles.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are small nanoparticles which possessed highly fluorescent and photoelectrochemical properties. The superior properties in fluorescent and adjustable parameters of the CQDs have shown promising application in mediating the optical limitations of traditional semiconductors [1,2]. Di et al. [3] investigated a novel nitrogen-doped CQDs for further modifying the BiOBr ultrathin nanosheets to obtain a better photocatalytic performance. The crucial role of N-CQDs has serving as a photocenter for harvesting light and separating charges with enhancing photocatalytic degradation of the pollutants. Current research on the CQDs/semiconductors hybrids are mainly efficient in control of the optical absorption, as well as in obtaining the higher quantum yields of fluorescent materials, and all of these could effectively enhance the performances of these semiconductors. Therefore, CQDs have been suggested as the intelligent candidate for bioimaging, photocatalysts (such as graphene quantum dots) [4], and so on. A series of photocatalytic experiments confirmed that the photocatalytic activity of introducing CQDs can be effectively improved under visible light irradiation, which may be attributed to their functional photoinduced electron-accepting properties [5]. Furthermore, the emissions of such CQDs covered a broad range of the visible region and extended into the near-infrared (NIR) region. CQDs have been combined with other semiconductors successfully contributed to enhance the photocatalytic activity both for contaminant degradation and hydrogen generation. Also forming the heterojunction by combining CQDs with different semiconductors, such as the recent researches on CQDs/semiconductors: CQDs/ZnO [6,7], CQDs/TiO2 [8,9], CQDs/BiOBr [10], CQDs/C3N4 [11–13], CQDs/CdS [14], CQDs/MoS2 [15], CQDs/ZnS [16], etc. However, the size and interface are also the critical issues for its modification on CQDs-based composites. The research for developing a novel type of CQDs-semiconductors based hybrid nano-materials could possess the distinctive properties and optical light absorption.

Rajabi group successfully prepared the QDs-semiconductor (such as ZnS), and the QDs-semiconductor could effectively remove the dyes under light irradiation, suggesting that the QDs-semiconductor can be used as one kind of photocatayst and possess higher photocatalytic activity [17,18]. We also recently reported the traditional II–VI semiconductor nano-materials [19–23], QDs CdSe with the narrow band gap energy Eg =1.75eV is regarded as a promising visible-light photocatalyst, which is sensitive to visible light and has been widely applied in photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants, solar cell, photoluminescence, etc. Although the QDs CdSe can be modified by coupling with other materials, there are still several issues, such as aggregation which could lead to decrease the contacted spots of the surface and increase the recombination rate of photo-holes and photo-electrons, limit the utilization of pure QDs CdSe nanoparticles. Therefore, efforts have been made to improve the photocatalytic performance of QDs CdSe. In particular, the QDs CdSe were prepared via hydrothermal method, and further modification with CQDs, to obtain high luminescent with better light absorption. The combination of CQDs and QDs CdSe allows for a quantitative chemical properties of the CQDs and QDs CdSe with enhanced photocatalytic performance. However, for the increasing demands of separating photo-generated e−/h+ pairs, the new type of functional materials such as carbon materials, act like migrating charge carriers. Among all the carbon materials, rGO possesses the unique 2D layer structure which achieved high specific surface area, high chemical stabilization, excellent electronic conductivity, etc [24–27]. which could be regarded as idea support for fabricating CQDs/CdSe-rGO materials. As a results, rGO could effectively distribute CQDs/CdSe hybrid; on the other side, rGO could enhance the transfer speed of photo-electrons, for these two aspects the fabricated CQDs/CdSe-rGO materials could improve the photocatalytic degradation rate of pollutant.

In this work, we devoted our attention to fabricate the CQDs-CdSe based hybrid nano-materials and further loading on rGO support. The photocatalytic performances were investigated by degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC·HCl), and the photocatalytic degradation conditions were also discussed. Lastly, we have described synthetic processes and the potential photocatalytic mechanism of these CQDs-CdSe-rGO hybrids.

QDs CdSe and graphene oxide (GO) were prepared as previous report before [28]. The CQDs were synthesized by the “bottom-up” approach which using a microwave heating route as follows: citric acid (1.0507g;C6H8O7, AR, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) and ethylenediamine (0.45g;C2H8N2, AR, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) was dissolved in deionized water (10mL) under vigorous stirring for 30min and then put into ultrasonic machine (Kunshan Ultrsonic Instruments Co. Ltd., KQ-400KDB: 40KHz, 400W) during 5min. The solution was transferred to a domestic microwave oven (Beijing XiangHu Science and Technology Development Co., Ltd., Computer Microwave-ultrasound-ultraviolet Combination Synthesizer, XH-300UL) at 800W for 10min. After cooling down to room temperature, the product, which was light-yellow and transparent, was subjected to dialysis with the molecular weight cutoff of 12,000 membrane for 24h in order to obtain CQDs. The resultant solution was sealed and stored in dry and cool place for subsequent synthesis. For the further synthesis of CQDs-CdSe-rGO, CdO precursor was obtained by 0.09134g of CdCl2·2.5H2O (AR, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) in 30mL of deionized water with 0.056mL of 3-mercaptopropionic acid (C3H6O2S, AR, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) under stirring for 5min. After that, the pH was regulated at a level of 7 by 1mol of NaOH (AR, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.). Meanwhile, NaHSe precursor was prepared as follows: 0.0592g of Se powders (AR, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) was mixed with 0.3783g of sodium borohydride (NaBH4, AR, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) and 5mL of deionized water while stirring under nitrogen atmosphere until the transparent sol was obtained. Then NaHSe precursor was quickly injected to CdO solution to form CdSe precursor. After 2h of stirring, the CdSe precursor was added to CQDs precursor, with 5wt% GO was added with stirring for 2h before the hydrothermal treatment and the GO can be reduced to rGO during the hydrothermal process. Lastly, the CQDs-CdSe-rGO photocatalysts were obtained. The formed processes of CQDs-CdSe-rGO nano-material is proposed in Scheme 1 .

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, MAC Science, Japan; scan range: 5–80° at a scan rate of 5°/min) was used to analyze the crystal structure of as-obtained photocatalysts. The micro-structures were observed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEM-2010, Japan). The adsorption light properties were determined by the diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450 Shimazu, Japan) equipped with an integrating sphere. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra for solid samples were performed by an F-4500 (Hitachi, Japan) photoluminescence detector.

The photocatalytic activities of as-prepared photocatalysts were evaluated by degradation of TC·HCl (20mgL−1) solutions under visible-light irradiation. A 350W xenon arc lamp was used as the light source in the photocatalytic reaction. Briefly, the photochemical reactor system contains 100mL of TC·HCl solution and 0.08g of photocatalysts with circulated water (25°C) to maintain a constant reactor temperature. The mixture was stirred under dark conditions for 30min to ensure that the adsorption–desorption balance was reached. Samples were taken from the suspension at a given 10min interval and centrifugally separated to remove photocatalysts for cyclic utilization in additions runs. The concentration of TC·HCl was measured via a UV–vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 357nm.

Fig. 1 shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the CdSe and CQDs/CdSe-rGO. For CdSe QDs, XRD peaks marked at 25.5°, 42.2°, and 49.9° are due to (111), (220), and (311) planes of the cubic zinc blende phase of CdSe which calculated the corresponding particles size are 8.63nm, 9.27nm, and 8.51nm (JCPDS 19-0191)[29–31], respectively, and the average particle size of as-obtained CdSe QDs is 8.80nm. Compared to CdSe QDs, the XRD patterns of CdSe after modification with CQDs and rGO are obviously changed, which may be ascribed to the introduction of CQDs on the surface. What’s more, the peak of rGO (locked at 2θ =26°) is not clear, the reason may be due to the low amount of rGO was introduced in the prepared processes.

Fig. 2 displays the TEM images of CQDs, CdSe QDs, CQDs/CdSe QDs, CQDs/CdSe QDs-rGO (5wt% GO), respectively. From Fig. 2a, it can be clearly found that the CQDs are uniform size and the particle size is around 3–5nm, which the results of as-obtained CQDs are similar to previous report [32]. We can see in Fig. 2b, a typical TEM image of CdSe QDs clusters shows serious aggregation and the particle size is about 12nm which is a little larger than the calculated datum. However, the introduction of CQDs is invisible for their limited size and the active spots on the surface (Fig. 2c). With the introduction of rGO, a relatively homogeneous distributions of CQDs/CdSe nanoparticles on rGO sheets are obtained, which may be because an efficiently interfacial electric field is established on the interface between rGO and the anchored CQDs/CdSe (Fig. 2d).

It is widely acknowledged that the photocatalytic activity is closely associated with the light absorption ability. Fig. 3 shows the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of CdSe, CQDs/CdSe, and CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO) photocatalysts. From Fig. 3, all of the as-prepared photocatalysts possess high light absorption ability and exhibit strong photoresponses from UV light region to visible light region (200nm–680nm). Compared the light absorption of CdSe, it can be also seen that the CQDs/CdSe and CQDs/CdSe-rGO show large scale red-shift, especially the CQDs/CdSe-rGO photocatalyst displays maximum red-shift, indicating that the CQDs/CdSe-rGO photocatalyst could utilize more visible light [33] and also may improve the generated yields of photo-holes and electrons. The enhanced light absorption is ascribed to CQDs with uniquely and excellently optical properties [34,35]. And the introduction of rGO has increased the charges migrating which could extend light absorption, the rGO could effectively enhance the separation of photo-electrons and holes.

It is well known that the photocatalytic activity is limited by the transfer of charges, in order to investigate the separation of photo-electrons and holes in the photocatalytic processes, photoluminescence (PL), generated by the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, is always used to determine the separation of photo-electrons and holes, and the PL intensity is closely related to the optical properties of photocatalysts [36–38]. Fig. 4 displays the PL spectra of CdSe QDs, CQDs/CdSe and CQDs/CdSe-rGO(5wt% GO). The intensity of pure CdSe QDs exhibit the highest emission. After coupling with CQDs, the PL emission intensity of CQDs/CdSe is notably lower than that of CdSe QDs, and the peak slightly blue shift, the reason may be due to that the introduced CQDs could enhance the light absorption and improve the charges transfer, further promote the separation rate of photo-electrons and holes, after modifying CODs, the result of lower intensity of PL is also similar to the previous report [39]. After introducing rGO, the rGO could cause the diffusion of the CQDs/CdSe which further enhanced the transfer rate of charges, the CQDs/CdSe-rGO photocatalyst therefore possesses lowest intensity of PL, indicating that the higher separation rate of photo-electrons and holes of as-prepared composite photocatalyst, and it also suggests that the CQDs/CdSe-rGO will produce highest photocatalytic activity.

The transient photocurrent serving as a powerful method to examine the charges transfer ability of as-prepared photocatalysts, and incident-photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE, CHI 660B, Shanghai, China) was used to analyze the photocurrents. Fig. 5 shows the transient photocurrent responses of CdSe QDs, CQDs/CdSe and CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO). The photocurrent densities were measured with the light on-off process (with a pulse of 50s and applied potential of 0.5V vs). Compared to photocurrent responses of CdSe QDs and CQDs/CdSe, it can be clearly seen that the photocurrent of CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO) is the highest, indicating the transfer rate of photoinduced carriers increased, which could improve separation of photogenerated e−/h+ pairs [40–43]. Herein, the result is consistent with the PL and UV–vis DRS results. Moreover, the photocurrent density of CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO) shows higher than that sum of CdSe QDs and CQDs/CdSe. Suggesting that the rGO coupled with CQDs/CdSe significantly enhances the transport of photo-electrons.

In order to investigate the photocatalytic activities of as-prepared composite photocatalysts, the TC·HCl was chose to the simulate antibiotic pollutants for photocatalytic degradation under visible light irradiation. The effect of the concentrations of introduced CQDs and rGO support on the activities of as-prepared composite photocatalysts have been investigated, the results show in Fig. 6 . From Fig. 6, it can be clearly seen that the concentration of TC·HCl decreases gradually as the reaction proceeded. Fig. 6a shows the effects of different amounts of CQDs modified the CdSe on the degradation of TC·HCl, it can be also easily found that CQDs/CdSe synthesized with 7mL CQDs exhibits relatively more activity than the other four samples which synthesized with 1mL, 3mL, 5mL and 9mL CQDs, respectively. The reason for enhancing the photocatalytic activities may be due to that the CQDs could promote the diffusion of CdSe and enhance the light absorption, further increase the transfer of charges in the photocatalytic processes. Moreover, CQDs can be excited under visible light which generated the photo-electrons and photo-holes, and further enhance the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. As shown in Fig. 6b, different amounts of GO support (from 1wt% to 9wt%) could obviously influence the photocatalytic degradation rate of TC·HCl, it clearly shows that at 5wt% GO the as-prepared CQDs/CdSe-rGO composite photocatalyst exhibits the highest activity. The reason for the proper content of rGO with the enhanced activity may be due the high conduction of rGO which could effectively transfer the photo-electrons and increase the separation of photoelectrons and holes; on the other side, the rGO may offer the more active spots which could adsorb the TC·HCl and further enhance the photocatalytic reactions. Indicating that composite photocatalyst of CQDs/CdSe-rGO has a higher photocatalytic activity than that of other photocatalysts, which further demonstrates that the proper introduction of CQDs and rGO support will increase the effective utilization of photoelectric properties.

However, the composite photocatalysts effective enhanced the photocatalytic degradation rate of TC·HCl, as we all known that the photocatalytic efficiency is usually limited by many factors. As for the conditions of solution, such as pH values and concentrations, obviously affect the removed efficiency of TC·HCl. Fig. 6c shows the removed efficiencies at different pH values (from 2 to 10) with 5wt% GO loaded CQDs/CdSe-rGO, and it is clearly seen that the pH value near the neutral condition possesses highest removed efficiency; corresponding that the low pH value and high pH value decrease the removed efficiencies of TC·HCl, especially the lowest removal of TC·HCl at pH value of 2. And the observation is similar to the report of Rajabi’s group [17]. As for the different concentrations of TC·HCl, the result shows in Fig. 6d, it is obviously found that the removed efficiency is higher at low concentration, in contrast the high concentration of TC·HCl causes the lower removed efficiency. In order to understand the photocatalytic degradation kinetics, the Langmuir–Hinshelwood kinetics model was used to further analyze the experimental data [4], and the result shows in Fig. 6e. From the results, we can find the pseudo-first order rate constants of 10mg/L TC·HCl (0.02836) is more than 3 times that of 50mg/L TC·HCl (0.00893), indicating that the lower concentration of TC·HCl could fast photocatalytic degradation under the same conditions. The stability of as-obtained photocatalyst was also investigated via recycled experiments, the results showed in Fig. 6f, the result shows the composite photocatalyst obviously exhibits high recyclability, the fifth cycle of degradation rate could reach 82.9%, compared with the first degradation, the degradation efficiency decreased less than 5%. Indicting that the as-prepared CQDs/CdSe-rGO 5wt% possesses higher photocatalytic activity and stability.

In order to understand the roles of active species in the photocatalytic degradation processes, the trapping experiments have been investigated by adding different trapping reagents (IPA, a quencher of OH; BQ, a quencher of O2
−; TEOA, a quencher of h+) [44–46], the results show in Fig. 7 . The photocatalytic degradation of TC·HCl with 0.08g of CQDs@CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO) and the addition of trapping reagents, when 1mmol of IPA was added in the photocatalytic process, the photocatalytic degradation rate could reach 68%, OH showed a little influence. However, the photocatalytic degradation rate of TC·HCl has clearly decreased to 38% with the addition of 1mmol of BQ and O2
− showed high effect. When the TEOA was introduced, the photodegradation rate of TC·HCl obviously drops to 17%, indicating that the h+ plays important role in the photocatalytic processes. From the trapping results, we can conclude that h+ and O2
− may be the major active species of CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO) in the photocatalytic reaction.

In order to confirm these active species, the Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) spectrometer (Brucker A300, Germany) was used to further certify the presence of OH, O2
− and 1O2 radicals of CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO) in the photocatalytic processes, the results show in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8a shows the spectrum of DMPO-O2
− test, it can be clearly found that the O2
− radicals are easy to form during the photocatalytic processes. The reason may be due to that the composite photocatalysts with CQDs and rGO could efficiently enhance charges transmission ability and show strong intensity of DMPO-O2
− signals. Fig. 8b shows the detection of DMPO−OH, there are a few characteristic peaks observed, suggesting low OH generated in the photocatalytic processes. And the results are consistent with the active species trapping experiments. Fig. 8c shows the spectrum of singlet oxygen (1O2) in the photocatalytic degradation processes. It can be obviously found that the TEMPONE signals are clearly generated under light irradiation. The reason may be due to that abundant O2
− can react to h+ to produce 1O2. Moreover, all the ESR signals are easy to detect under visible light irradiation, in contrast, no ESR signals are detected without irradiation. Indicating that the introduction of CQDs and rGO could effectively enhance charges separation under light irradiation, which could produce the active species and increase the photocatalytic degradation rates of TC·HCl [47–49].

According to the results of characterizations and photocatalytic degradation analysis, the mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of TC·HCl with CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO) is proposed and the processes follow Fig. 9 . It can be clearly found that CQDs/CdSe-rGO (5wt% GO) is easily excited under visible light irradiation, first the CdSe is excited and generated the photo-electrons and photo-holes, the photo-electrons transfer to the surface of rGO via CQDs, and the photo-electrons react to O2 and form the O2
− active species; meanwhile, the CQDs can be also excited under visible light irradiation and generated the photo-electrons and photo-holes which enhanced the yields of OH and O2
−. On the other side, the O2
− species can react with photo-holes and generate the singlet oxygen (1O2). And all the active species could lead the degradation of TC·HCl.

The CQDs modified CdSe loaded rGO composite photocatalysts were successfully prepared via hydrothermal method. The enhanced performances of as-prepared photocatalysts were confirmed by the characterizations and the photocatalytic degradation processes. Followed the results, the CQDs and rGO were introduced into the composite and effectively improved the yields of active species which included OH, O2
− and 1O2, and further enhanced the photocatalytic activities. The photocatalytic degradation rate of TC·HCl could reach 90% in 60min visible light irradiation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21576125), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant No.BK20140532), Six talent peaks project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No.XCL-014), Zhenjiang Science & Technology Program (Grant No.SH2016012).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colloid. Surf. A: Physicochem. Eng. Asp.
